name: The Sun
subtitle: Center of the Solar System · Our parent star
overview: The Sun contains 99.86% of all mass in the Solar System. Every second, 600 million tonnes of hydrogen fuse into helium in its core, releasing energy that takes 100,000 years to migrate to the surface — and then just 8 minutes 20 seconds to cross the 150 million km to Earth. When it dies, it will swell into a red giant large enough to engulf Earth's orbit.
features: Sunspots: regions of intense magnetic activity cooler than the surrounding photosphere, following an 11-year activity cycle
Solar flares: bursts of radiation intense enough to disrupt Earth's power grids and communications (Carrington Event, 1859)
Coronal Mass Ejections: billion-tonne plasma clouds hurled at 1–3 million km/h — the cause of auroras on Earth
The heliosphere: a 200+ AU bubble of solar wind that shields the entire Solar System from galactic cosmic rays
Differential rotation: the equator rotates in 25 days, the poles in 35 — creating the magnetic stress that drives solar activity
missions: Parker Solar Probe (NASA, 2018–present): diving within 6.9 million km of the Sun's surface — closer than any spacecraft in history; the fastest human-made object
Solar Orbiter (ESA/NASA, 2020–present): first direct images of the Sun's poles and discovery of 'campfire' micro-flares
SOHO (ESA/NASA, 1995–present): 30 years of continuous solar monitoring from the L1 Lagrange point
Hinode (JAXA, 2006–present): high-resolution study of the Sun's magnetic field and corona
fact: A photon created in the Sun's core by nuclear fusion takes between 10,000 and 170,000 years to escape through the dense plasma — bumping millions of times before reaching the surface. Then it crosses 150 million km of vacuum to reach your eye in just 8 minutes. The sunlight you see is ancient.

name: Mercury
subtitle: Innermost planet · Smallest in the Solar System
overview: Mercury is a world of contradictions: closest to the Sun yet not the hottest planet; the smallest planet yet with proportionally the largest iron core; extreme temperature swings of over 600°C between its scorching day side and freezing night side. Its 3:2 spin-orbit resonance — rotating exactly 1.5 times per orbit — creates a peculiar 'day' lasting 176 Earth days, longer than its year.

stats.label: Atmosphere
stats.value: Exosphere only: Na, O, H, He

features: 3:2 spin-orbit resonance: Mercury rotates exactly 3 times for every 2 orbits — a gravitational lock creating a day longer than its year
Enormous iron core: 85% of Mercury's radius is iron core — the largest core-to-planet ratio of any planet; possibly caused by a giant impact that stripped the mantle
Caloris Basin: one of the Solar System's largest impact craters — 1,550 km across, created by a Pluto-sized body early in Solar System history
Water ice at poles: radar confirms ice in permanently shadowed polar craters despite surface temperatures of 430°C on the sun-facing side
Magnetic field: weak but measurable (1% of Earth's), surprising for a world thought too small for a liquid core; first detected by Mariner 10
missions: Mariner 10 (NASA, 1974–75): first spacecraft to visit Mercury; mapped ~45% of the surface across three flybys
MESSENGER (NASA, 2011–2015): first Mercury orbiter; mapped the entire surface; confirmed water ice at poles; discovered ancient volcanic activity
BepiColombo (ESA/JAXA, launched 2018, arrival 2025): two-spacecraft mission — the most sophisticated Mercury mission ever; will study interior, surface, and magnetosphere
fact: A year on Mercury (88 Earth days) is shorter than its solar day (176 Earth days). An astronaut standing on Mercury's surface would see the Sun rise, stop, reverse direction briefly, then continue rising — a result of its unusual spin-orbit resonance and highly elliptical orbit.

name: Venus
subtitle: Earth's twin in size · Hellish runaway greenhouse world
overview: Venus is Earth's closest sibling in size and composition, yet it evolved into a nightmarish world. A runaway greenhouse effect raised surface temperatures to 462°C — hot enough to melt lead — uniformly across day and night, poles and equator. Venus rotates backwards (retrograde) so slowly that one Venusian day (243 Earth days) outlasts its year (225 days). On Venus, the Sun rises in the west.

stats.label: Atmosphere
stats.value: 96.5% CO₂, 3.5% N₂; H₂SO₄ clouds at 45–70 km

features: Runaway greenhouse effect: CO₂ traps heat so effectively that despite being 40M km farther from the Sun than Mercury, Venus is 30°C hotter
Sulfuric acid cloud layers: at 45–70 km altitude, clouds of concentrated H₂SO₄ and droplets create permanent global overcast
Retrograde rotation: Venus spins clockwise when viewed from above — opposite to Earth and most planets; origin possibly a catastrophic ancient collision
Active volcanism: Magellan radar revealed thousands of volcanoes; 2023 Magellan archival data confirmed active volcanic vents observed changing shape
Phosphine controversy (2020): phosphine (PH₃) detected in the cloud layer — on Earth only produced by biology or industry — reignited debate about cloud-layer microbial life
missions: Venera program (USSR, 1970–1985): 10 spacecraft survived landing; Venera 13 (1982) returned the first color images of Venus's surface; probes survived 23–127 minutes before succumbing to heat and pressure
Magellan (NASA, 1990–1994): synthetic aperture radar mapped 98% of the surface through dense clouds; revealed a young, geologically active world
Akatsuki (JAXA, 2015–present): orbiting Venus studying atmospheric dynamics and gravity waves
Upcoming (~2030s): DAVINCI+ (NASA) — atmospheric descent probe; VERITAS (NASA) — radar mapper; EnVision (ESA) — comprehensive orbiter
fact: The Soviet Venera landers were built specifically to survive Venus — with titanium shells, cooling systems, and crush-proof designs. Even so, the longest any lander survived was 127 minutes. Every spacecraft ever sent to Venus's surface has been destroyed by the environment within 2 hours.

name: Earth
subtitle: The Blue Marble · Only known life-bearing world
overview: Earth is the largest and densest of the four rocky planets, and the only world known to harbor life. Its unique combination of liquid water, plate tectonics, a large stabilizing Moon, a protective magnetic field, and an oxygen-rich atmosphere has sustained a biosphere for 3.8 billion years. Currently home to an estimated 8.7 million species — including 8.1 billion humans altering the planet at a pace visible from orbit.
features: Plate tectonics: Earth's crust is divided into 15+ plates that drift at fingernail-growth speed — creating mountains, triggering earthquakes, recycling carbon; unique in the Solar System
Magnetic field: generated by the churning molten iron outer core, the magnetosphere deflects solar wind that would otherwise strip the atmosphere over millions of years
The Moon: formed ~4.5 billion years ago from a Mars-sized body (Theia) colliding with proto-Earth; stabilizes Earth's 23.5° axial tilt, preventing catastrophic climate swings
Oxygenated atmosphere: produced entirely by cyanobacteria 2.4 billion years ago (Great Oxidation Event) — an irreversible planetary transformation driven by life
CO₂ at 422 ppm (2024): highest concentration in 800,000 years of ice-core records; global average temperature +1.2°C above pre-industrial baseline and rising
missions: ~9,000 active satellites (2024): monitoring climate, enabling communications, navigation (GPS/Galileo/BeiDou), and Earth observation
ISS (1998–present): 26 years of continuous human habitation in low Earth orbit across 270+ residents from 20 countries
Copernicus Programme (ESA): Sentinel satellite fleet providing free, open climate and environmental data since 2014
GRACE-FO (NASA/DLR): measuring ice sheet mass loss and groundwater depletion by tracking Earth's gravitational variations
fact: Earth's inner core is as hot as the Sun's surface (~5,700°C), yet solid — kept from melting by the immense pressure of the overlying planet. The inner core spins slightly faster than the rest of Earth and completes an extra rotation approximately every 400 years.

name: The Moon
subtitle: Earth's only moon · Closest extraterrestrial body to humanity
overview: The Moon formed approximately 4.5 billion years ago when a Mars-sized protoplanet called Theia collided with early Earth, ejecting material that coalesced into the Moon. Tidally locked to Earth, it always shows the same face. The Moon is slowly receding — moving 3.8 cm farther away each year (the same rate your fingernails grow). When it formed, it was 10× closer and appeared 10× larger in Earth's sky, raising ocean tides hundreds of meters high.
features: Tidal locking: gravitational forces have synchronized the Moon's rotation with its orbit — one face permanently toward Earth, one permanently away
Water ice confirmed at both poles (LCROSS mission, 2009): permanently shadowed craters near the poles never receive sunlight; ice trapped there for billions of years is a critical future resource
Lunar maria: vast dark plains formed by ancient lava floods 3–4 billion years ago; visible from Earth as the 'Man in the Moon'; cover 16% of the surface
Moonquakes: still seismically active; deep moonquakes (~700–1,200 km down) triggered by Earth's tidal forces; shallow quakes possibly from thermal contraction
Earth's axial stabilizer: without the Moon's gravitational influence, Earth's axial tilt could vary chaotically between 0° and 85° over millions of years — catastrophic for complex life
missions: Apollo program (NASA, 1969–1972): 12 astronauts walked on the Moon across 6 landings; 382 kg of samples returned; retroreflectors still used today for laser ranging
Chang'e 5 (China, 2020): returned 1.7 kg of lunar samples — youngest lunar samples ever retrieved (1.96 billion years old)
Chandrayaan-3 (ISRO, 2023): India became the fourth nation to land on the Moon and the first to land at the south pole
Artemis program (NASA, 2024+): returning humans to the Moon — first woman and first person of color to land; establishing Lunar Gateway orbital station
fact: Scientists fire lasers at retroreflectors left by Apollo astronauts every night, measuring the Moon's distance to within a few millimeters. This confirms the Moon is drifting away — and that in ~600 million years it will be too far to cause total solar eclipses, ending one of Earth's most spectacular astronomical phenomena.

name: Mars
subtitle: The Red Planet · Most explored world beyond Earth
overview: Mars was once a warm, wet world with a thicker atmosphere, flowing rivers, and possibly life. Today it is a frozen desert, its ancient oceans long gone, magnetic field collapsed, atmosphere stripped by solar wind. Yet the evidence of its watery past is written across its geology — and it remains the most explored planet beyond Earth and humanity's most likely destination for colonization.

stats.label: Atmosphere
stats.value: 95% CO₂, 2.6% N₂, 1.9% Ar; pressure 0.6% of Earth's

features: Olympus Mons: tallest volcano in the Solar System — 21.9 km high, 600 km wide (the area of France); so wide its rim curves below the horizon from its own summit
Valles Marineris: canyon system 4,000 km long, 200 km wide, 7 km deep — if on Earth, it would span the entire continental United States
Subsurface lake: MARSIS radar (Mars Express) detected what appears to be a liquid water lake ~20 km wide beneath the south polar ice cap at −68°C, possibly kept liquid by dissolved salts
Ancient habitability confirmed: Curiosity found organic molecules and evidence of an ancient freshwater lake in Gale Crater — conditions suitable for microbial life ~3.5 billion years ago
Ingenuity helicopter: made 72+ flights on Mars — the first powered, controlled flight on another planet; far exceeded its 5-flight mission plan
missions: Curiosity (NASA, 2012–present): nuclear-powered rover; confirmed ancient habitability; discovered organic molecules and seasonal methane; still active after 12+ years
Perseverance (NASA, 2021–present): collecting core samples for future return to Earth; deployed Ingenuity helicopter; searching for biosignatures in an ancient river delta
Mars Sample Return (NASA/ESA, ~2030s): will retrieve Perseverance's samples — the first samples returned from another planet
Tianwen-1 / Zhurong (China, 2021): China's first Mars mission; successfully landed the Zhurong rover; confirmed subsurface layered structures
fact: Phobos, Mars's larger moon, is spiraling inward at 1.8 cm per year and will either crash into Mars or break apart into a ring system in approximately 30–50 million years. Deimos, the smaller moon, is slowly escaping Mars's gravity and will eventually drift away.

name: Jupiter
subtitle: King of planets · Largest body in the Solar System
overview: Jupiter contains more than twice the mass of all other planets combined. Its gravity has shaped the entire Solar System — sculpting the asteroid belt, deflecting comets away from the inner Solar System (protecting Earth), and possibly flinging early rocky material inward to seed Earth with water. Had Jupiter been 80× more massive, it would have ignited as a star, and our Solar System would look completely different.
features: Great Red Spot: an anticyclonic storm larger than Earth, raging continuously for at least 350 years; currently ~13,000 km wide and shrinking — was 40,000 km wide in the 19th century

Europa: ice-covered moon with a global liquid ocean containing twice as much water as all Earth's oceans; tidal heating keeps it liquid; considered the Solar System's best candidate for extraterrestrial life
Io: the most volcanically active body in the Solar System with 400+ active volcanoes; tidal forces from Jupiter generate more heat than all Earth's geothermal and radioactive sources combined
Ganymede: largest moon in the Solar System (bigger than Mercury and Pluto); has its own magnetic field and a subsurface saltwater ocean sandwiched between ice layers
Radiation belts: Jupiter's magnetic field is 20,000× Earth's strength, trapping charged particles in belts of radiation lethal to electronics; visiting spacecraft must be hardened
missions: Galileo (NASA, 1995–2003): first spacecraft to orbit Jupiter; discovered Europa's ocean; deployed the only probe ever sent into a gas giant's atmosphere
Juno (NASA, 2016–present): revealed Jupiter's internal structure, polar cyclones, and surprisingly lumpy interior; extended mission through 2025
Europa Clipper (NASA, 2024 launch): will make 49 close flybys of Europa, assessing the ocean's chemistry and habitability
JUICE (ESA, 2023 launch): will orbit Ganymede and study the Galilean moons; arrives Jupiter in 2031
fact: Jupiter's magnetic field extends 600–700 million km into space — past Saturn's orbit. If you could see it with naked eyes from Earth, it would appear larger than the full Moon. The aurora at Jupiter's poles releases more energy in a few minutes than all of Earth's lightning combined in a year.

name: Saturn
subtitle: The Jewel of the Solar System · Lord of the rings
overview: Saturn's rings are among the most beautiful structures in the Solar System — yet they are surprisingly young (100–400 million years old, long after dinosaurs appeared on Earth) and temporary. They are actively 'raining' water into Saturn's atmosphere at a rate that will erase them entirely within 100 million years. Saturn itself is so large it could contain 763 Earths, yet so light it would float on water.

stats.label: Ring span
stats.value: 282,000 km wide, ~10 m to 1 km thick

features: Rings: 93% water ice, 7% rocky material; individual particles range from dust grains to house-sized boulders; the main rings span 282,000 km but are only 10 metres to 1 km thick

Enceladus: active cryovolcanism shoots jets of water, organic molecules, and hydrogen 200 km into space from 'tiger stripe' fissures; Cassini flew through and tasted complex organics, salts, and silica — all requirements for life

Titan: the only moon in the Solar System with a dense atmosphere (1.5× Earth's pressure) and stable surface liquids — lakes and seas of liquid methane and ethane; Huygens probe landed on its surface in 2005
North polar hexagon: a geometric hexagonal storm system 32,000 km across at Saturn's north pole, stable for decades — a fluid dynamics phenomenon not fully understood
Ring rain: rings are losing mass into Saturn's atmosphere at 400–2,800 kg per second; the rings will be gone in 100 million years
missions: Pioneer 11 (1979) and Voyager 1 & 2 (1980–81): first close-up views of Saturn's rings, moons, and atmosphere

Cassini-Huygens (NASA/ESA/ASI, 2004–2017): 13 years in Saturn orbit; Huygens probe landed on Titan; discovered Enceladus geysers; found complex organics — one of humanity's greatest planetary science missions
Dragonfly (NASA, ~2028 launch, 2034 arrival): nuclear-powered rotorcraft will fly across Titan's surface, sampling chemistry at multiple sites to search for prebiotic molecules
fact: Cassini detected molecular hydrogen (H₂) in Enceladus's plumes — produced by water reacting with hot rock on the ocean floor. On Earth, this exact process (hydrothermal vents) supports entire ecosystems without sunlight. Enceladus may be doing the same right now.

name: Uranus
subtitle: The tilted world · Rolling through space sideways
overview: Uranus is the Solar System's 'sideways planet' — tilted 97.77° on its axis, meaning it essentially rolls along its orbital path like a ball. Each pole experiences 42 years of continuous sunlight followed by 42 years of darkness. Despite being closer to the Sun than Neptune, Uranus's atmosphere is actually colder — it is the only giant planet that radiates almost no internal heat, a mystery not yet fully explained.

stats.label: Composition
stats.value: Water, methane, ammonia ices + H₂/He outer layer

features: Extreme axial tilt: caused by a collision with an Earth-mass protoplanet ~4 billion years ago; one pole points almost directly at the Sun for 42 years, then the other
Coldest atmosphere in the Solar System: −224°C, colder than Neptune despite being closer to the Sun — possibly because its interior is unusually structured, trapping heat rather than radiating it

Miranda's cliff: Verona Rupes, a cliff on Uranus's moon Miranda, is ~20 km tall — the tallest known cliff in the Solar System; gravity is so low a rock dropped from the top would take 12 minutes to reach the bottom
Internal heat mystery: Uranus radiates almost no excess heat from its interior — unlike all other giant planets; its interior may have a thermally insulating layer preventing convection
Methane absorption: methane in the upper atmosphere absorbs red light and reflects blue-green light, giving Uranus its distinctive cyan color
missions: Voyager 2 (NASA, 1986): the only spacecraft to visit Uranus; a brief flyby of just hours; discovered 10 moons, 2 rings, and a bizarre magnetic field tilted 59° from its rotation axis
No further missions in 38 years — Uranus remains the least explored giant planet

Uranus Orbiter and Probe (NASA, proposed ~2031 launch): identified as the highest-priority large planetary mission in the 2023–2032 Decadal Survey; would orbit for 4+ years and release an atmospheric probe
fact: Uranus and Neptune are often called 'ice giants' but their interiors are not frozen. At depth, water, ammonia, and methane exist as a hot, dense fluid at pressures millions of times Earth's atmospheric pressure — possibly generating strange forms of matter like 'superionic water' where oxygen atoms form a crystalline lattice while hydrogen ions flow freely.

name: Neptune
subtitle: Farthest known planet · Realm of extreme winds
overview: Neptune was the first planet discovered through mathematical prediction rather than observation. Astronomers noticed Uranus's orbit was being perturbed, calculated where an unknown body must be — and found Neptune almost exactly where predicted. Despite receiving only 1/900th of Earth's sunlight, it has the most violent weather in the Solar System. Its largest moon, Triton, is being slowly torn apart by Neptune's gravity and will eventually form a new ring system.

stats.label: Wind speed
stats.value: Up to 2,100 km/h — fastest in the Solar System

features: Supersonic winds: 2,100 km/h winds driven by internal heat — mysterious given Neptune receives such little sunlight; internal energy powers weather far more than solar energy
Triton: orbits Neptune backwards (retrograde) and is the only large moon in the Solar System to do so — it's almost certainly a captured Kuiper Belt Object, like Pluto's cousin
Triton's doom: tidal deceleration is pulling Triton inward at ~10 cm/year; in 100–375 million years it will cross the Roche limit and shatter into a spectacular ring system rivaling Saturn's
Triton's geysers: despite a surface temperature of −235°C, Triton has active nitrogen geysers — solar heating vaporizes nitrogen trapped beneath its translucent ice, launching plumes 8 km high
Internal heat engine: Neptune radiates 2.6× more heat than it receives from the Sun — the source of its extreme atmospheric dynamics and driving force behind its winds
missions: Voyager 2 (NASA, 1989): the only spacecraft to visit Neptune; 12,000 km closest approach; discovered the Great Dark Spot, active geysers on Triton, 6 new moons, and complete ring arcs
No further missions since 1989 — Neptune has been observed only by telescopes for 35 years
Neptune Odyssey (proposed): a 12-year journey to orbit Neptune and land on Triton; would also study the Kuiper Belt en route; under consideration for the next Decadal Survey
fact: Neptune has completed only one full orbit around the Sun since it was discovered in 1846. A Neptunian year is 164.8 Earth years — everyone who has ever lived has done so within less than two Neptunian years. The radio signal from Voyager 2's Neptune flyby took 4 hours 6 minutes to reach Earth.
